g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 1. Today we explore how processes and threads coordinate in parallel computing.
In parallel computing, without coordination, race conditions occur when multiple entities access shared resources simultaneously.
g.text: Parallel execution without coordination leads to
Race Conditions → Data Corruption → Incorrect Results

g.data-narration: The Critical Section is the code segment where shared resources are accessed and requires mutual exclusion.
g.text: A code segment where shared resources (variables, files) are accessed

g.data-narration: A solution to the critical section problem must satisfy three conditions: Mutual Exclusion, Progress, and Bounded Waiting.
g.text: Three Conditions for Critical Section Solution
If none in CS & want entry,

g.data-narration: Locks (Mutexes) are the simplest synchronization mechanism. A thread must acquire the lock before entering the critical section.
g.text: Simplest mechanism - like a token granting permission to enter CS

g.data-narration: Semaphores, invented by Dijkstra, are integer variables with atomic wait(P) and signal(V) operations for synchronization.
g.text: Integer variable with two atomic operations: wait(P) and signal(V)

g.data-narration: Binary semaphores (0 or 1) act like mutexes for mutual exclusion. Counting semaphores control multiple resource instances.
g.text: Value: 0 or 1 | Used for Mutual Exclusion
Value: 0 to N | Controls resource pool access

g.data-narration: Monitors are high-level language constructs that encapsulate shared data and procedures, allowing only one thread inside at a time.
g.text: Programming language construct encapsulating shared data + procedures

g.data-narration: Shared Memory allows threads to communicate by reading/writing to shared variables. It's fast but requires explicit synchronization.
g.text: Threads communicate by reading/writing to shared variables in memory

g.data-narration: Message Passing uses system calls to send/receive messages between processes. Slower but naturally avoids shared state issues.
g.text: Processes send and receive messages via system calls to communicate

g.data-narration: Shared Memory is fast but requires synchronization. Message Passing avoids shared state but has copying overhead.
g.text: ⚡ Fast | ⚠ Explicit sync needed | Best for threads

g.data-narration: To summarize: Use locks/mutexes for mutual exclusion, semaphores for resource control, monitors for high-level safety, and choose communication based on your architecture.
This knowledge connects to OpenMP synchronization in Module 4, MPI in Module 3, and CUDA thread synchronization in Module 5.
